title: The program that taught itself to play 49 Atari games
tldr: A single program from Google DeepMind learned to play 49 classic Atari games from nothing but the screen pixels and the score, reaching or beating a skilled human on about half of them without ever being told the rules.
citation: Mnih, Kavukcuoglu, Silver et al. (2015), "Human-level control through deep reinforcement learning", Nature 518, 529-533

title: The chip that did in minutes what a supercomputer couldn't
tldr: Google's 53-qubit chip finished a carefully chosen task in about 200 seconds that they argued would take a top supercomputer thousands of years, the first hard evidence that a quantum machine can outrun every classical one.
citation: Arute, Arya, Babbush et al. (2019), "Quantum supremacy using a programmable superconducting processor", Nature 574, 505-510

title: The stock market jumps around way too much
tldr: Shiller showed the stock market swings far more wildly than the actual cash companies later paid out could ever justify, which poked a deep hole in the idea that prices simply reflect cold, rational forecasts.
citation: Shiller (1981), "Do Stock Prices Move Too Much to be Justified by Subsequent Changes in Dividends?", American Economic Review 71(3), 421-436

title: The runner's high isn't an endorphin thing
tldr: When mice ran until they felt calmer and tougher to pain, blocking endorphins did nothing, but blocking their natural cannabis-like molecules erased the glow.
citation: Fuss, Steinle, Bindila, Auer, Kirchherr, Lutz & Gass (2015), "A runner's high depends on cannabinoid receptors in mice", PNAS 112(42), 13105-13108

title: The real study behind the 10,000-hour rule
tldr: When researchers tracked the practice histories of violin students at a top music school, the most accomplished players had quietly logged thousands more hours of focused, effortful practice than their good-but-not-elite peers — the study that later got squashed into the catchy and misleading '10,000-hour rule.'
citation: K. Anders Ericsson, Ralf Th. Krampe & Clemens Tesch-Römer (1993), "The Role of Deliberate Practice in the Acquisition of Expert Performance," Psychological Review 100(3), 363-406

title: Why reading on a screen before bed wrecks your sleep
tldr: When researchers had people read a glowing tablet for a few hours before bed, their bodies pumped out about half as much of the sleep hormone melatonin, they took longer to drift off, and they woke up groggier the next morning — even after a full eight hours in bed.
citation: Anne-Marie Chang, Daniel Aeschbach, Jeanne F. Duffy & Charles A. Czeisler (2015), "Evening use of light-emitting eReaders negatively affects sleep, circadian timing, and next-morning alertness," PNAS 112(4), 1232-1237

title: The afternoon a contest entry kicked off the AI era
tldr: For decades, getting a computer to recognize what's in a photo was painfully hard. In 2012, three researchers entered a giant image contest with a single big neural network trained on graphics cards, and it beat everyone else by a stunning margin. That one result is why almost every AI system you use today exists.
citation: Alex Krizhevsky, Ilya Sutskever & Geoffrey E. Hinton (2012), "ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks" (AlexNet), Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS) 25, 1097-1105

title: The one-letter swap that made mRNA vaccines possible
tldr: Lab-made mRNA kept setting off a violent immune alarm — until two researchers swapped one of its four chemical letters for a slightly altered version, and the body stopped treating it as an invader. That fix is the reason the COVID mRNA vaccines work, and it won a Nobel Prize 18 years later.
citation: Katalin Karikó, Michael Buckstein, Houping Ni & Drew Weissman (2005), "Suppression of RNA Recognition by Toll-Like Receptors: The Impact of Nucleoside Modification and the Evolutionary Origin of RNA," Immunity 23(2), 165-175

title: Why the mug in your hand is suddenly worth more
tldr: Hand someone a coffee mug and they instantly demand about twice as much to sell it as they'd have paid to buy it — a simple classroom experiment showing that owning a thing, even for a minute, reshapes what you think it's worth.
citation: Daniel Kahneman, Jack L. Knetsch & Richard H. Thaler (1990), "Experimental Tests of the Endowment Effect and the Coase Theorem," Journal of Political Economy 98(6), 1325-1348

title: Finding the lock that THC fits
tldr: Researchers at the U.S. National Institutes of Health found and copied the gene for a brain receptor that THC plugs into — the first hard proof the body comes built with a docking site for cannabis, and the discovery that opened up the whole field of cannabinoid biology.
citation: Lisa A. Matsuda, Stephen J. Lolait, Michael J. Brownstein, Alice C. Young & Tom I. Bonner (1990), "Structure of a cannabinoid receptor and functional expression of the cloned cDNA," Nature 346(6284), 561-564

title: Why spreading out your study time beats cramming
tldr: Five researchers pooled more than a century of memory experiments and found the same thing over and over: take a fixed amount of study time and spread it across separate sessions, and you remember far more than if you pack it all into one sitting.
citation: Nicholas J. Cepeda, Harold Pashler, Edward Vul, John T. Wixted & Doug Rohrer (2006), "Distributed Practice in Verbal Recall Tasks: A Review and Quantitative Synthesis," Psychological Bulletin 132(3), 354-380

title: Breathing your way to a quieter immune system
tldr: Dutch researchers took the breathing-and-cold method made famous by Wim Hof, trained a group of volunteers in it for ten days, then injected everyone with a bacterial toxin — and the trained group dialed down their own inflammation and felt noticeably less sick.
citation: Matthijs Kox, Lucas T. van Eijk, Jelle Zwaag, Joanne van den Wildenberg, Fred C. G. J. Sweep, Johannes G. van der Hoeven & Peter Pickkers (2014), "Voluntary activation of the sympathetic nervous system and attenuation of the innate immune response in humans," Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences 111(20), 7379-7384

title: The machine that taught itself to play Go
tldr: Go was supposed to be a decade away from machines because there are more possible games than atoms in the universe — then a program called AlphaGo learned the game from human matches and from playing itself, and beat a professional champion five games to zero.
citation: Silver et al. (2016), "Mastering the game of Go with deep neural networks and tree search", Nature 529, 484-489

title: The day we heard two black holes collide
tldr: A hundred years after Einstein predicted that violent events in space send ripples through spacetime itself, two giant detectors caught one — the faint echo of two black holes smashing together over a billion light-years away — opening a brand-new way to listen to the universe.
citation: B. P. Abbott et al. (LIGO Scientific Collaboration and Virgo Collaboration) (2016), "Observation of Gravitational Waves from a Binary Black Hole Merger," Physical Review Letters 116, 061102

title: The math that made diversification a free lunch
tldr: A 25-year-old grad student showed that what matters is not how good your individual stocks are but how they move together — and that mixing assets wisely can cut risk without costing you return.
citation: Markowitz, H. (1952), "Portfolio Selection," The Journal of Finance 7(1), 77-91

title: The trial that turned CBD into medicine
tldr: A rigorous trial showed that a purified ingredient from cannabis roughly halved seizures in children with a severe epilepsy — the evidence that moved CBD from folk remedy to FDA-approved drug.
citation: Devinsky et al. (2017), "Trial of Cannabidiol for Drug-Resistant Seizures in the Dravet Syndrome," New England Journal of Medicine 376(21)

title: Why the worst performers are the most confident
tldr: Two psychologists tested people on humor, grammar, and logic, then asked everyone how well they thought they'd done. The people who scored at the very bottom were sure they'd scored near the top — because the same gaps in skill that made them fail also robbed them of the ability to see that they had failed.
citation: Kruger & Dunning (1999), "Unskilled and Unaware of It: How Difficulties in Recognizing One's Own Incompetence Lead to Inflated Self-Assessments," Journal of Personality and Social Psychology 77(6)

title: Your brain power-washes itself while you sleep
tldr: Your brain has its own plumbing system that flushes out waste, and a study in mice showed it switches into high gear during sleep — the spaces between brain cells widen and a tide of fluid rinses away gunk, including the same sticky protein that builds up in Alzheimer's.
citation: Xie et al. (2013), "Sleep Drives Metabolite Clearance from the Adult Brain," Science 342(6156)

title: The AI that cracked a 50-year-old biology puzzle
tldr: Proteins are tiny chains that fold into intricate 3D shapes, and that shape is everything — but figuring it out used to take years in a lab. An AI called AlphaFold learned to predict the shape from the recipe in minutes, solving a problem biologists had chased for half a century.
citation: Jumper et al. (2021), "Highly accurate protein structure prediction with AlphaFold," Nature 596(7873)

title: Why testing yourself beats re-reading
tldr: The act of pulling a fact out of your head — not putting it back in — is what makes it stick. This is the study that proves quizzing beats reviewing.
citation: Roediger & Karpicke (2006), "Test-Enhanced Learning," Psychological Science 17(3)

title: The eight-page paper that built ChatGPT
tldr: A 2017 paper threw out the standard way machines read text — one word at a time — and let the model weigh every word against every other word at once. That design, the Transformer, is the 'T' in GPT and the engine under almost every AI you use today.
citation: Vaswani et al. (2017), "Attention Is All You Need," Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 30 (NeurIPS 2017)

title: The mice that ate junk on a schedule and stayed lean
tldr: Mice eating the exact same high-fat calories stayed lean and healthy when they could only eat within an 8-hour window — the clock did the work that calorie-cutting usually gets credit for.
citation: Hatori et al. (2012), "Time-Restricted Feeding without Reducing Caloric Intake Prevents Metabolic Diseases in Mice Fed a High-Fat Diet," Cell Metabolism 15(6), 848-860

title: The brain makes its own cannabis
tldr: Your brain makes its own version of the active ingredient in cannabis. Scientists found it, and named it after the Sanskrit word for bliss.
citation: Devane et al. (1992), "Isolation and Structure of a Brain Constituent That Binds to the Cannabinoid Receptor," Science 258(5090)

title: Why a loss hurts twice as much as a win feels good
tldr: People don't weigh risk the way economic theory says they should. Losses sting about twice as hard as equivalent gains feel good — and that single asymmetry rewrote economics.
citation: Kahneman & Tversky (1979), "Prospect Theory: An Analysis of Decision under Risk," Econometrica 47(2)

title: The paper that turned a bacterial defense into gene-editing scissors
tldr: Bacteria have an immune system that slices up invading viruses at a precise spot. This paper showed you could swap in your own targeting instructions — turning that slicer into a tool that cuts any DNA sequence you choose.
citation: Jinek, Chylinski, Fonfara, Hauer, Doudna & Charpentier (2012), "A Programmable Dual-RNA–Guided DNA Endonuclease in Adaptive Bacterial Immunity," Science 337(6096)
